speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Reverend Ernest Levy. And this tape number four. You were warned somehow clandestinely to save food, because you were going to leave Wustegiersdorf. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were marched from Wustegiersdorf into the Silesian Mountains and hills-- 1,000 people. After a couple of days, half of the column of the group was taken to Flossenburg concentration camp. And I remember looking down. We went up a kind of serpentine road, yes? And  we looked down. And we saw the other half going left, taking that turn. And I waved to [INAUDIBLE]. I waved to him. And he waved back-- the man who gave me the little food the last moment. And they went to Flossenberg. After the war, he told me-- [? Levy ?] survived the war. He told me the journey to Flossenberg was absolutely indescribable. They ate each other. They were 120 people pushed into-- it-- he couldn't even start describing it. That was the half of us going to Flossenburg. We were marched over the the hills  and mountain of Silesia day after day. But by that time, I made friends with  a German guard, Helmut, who was always trying to be near my side, walking near to me. I spoke German. He was a lovely chap. He gave me some food sometimes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by that time. And we were marched and marched. And then we ended up-- the weather became impossible. It was winter-- snow and ice. And it was-- deteriorated so much that we  had to stop and put into different, different farms. And we ended up on a farm on the left. There was a big farm, a Max was looking, a man in his late '60s. You know, [? this ?] [? big ?] man in the Wellington boots looking at us. Eventually, we were put into a barn, to a barn-- 200 of us-- no windows, no nothing. But we heard him pleading. Max was pleading with the Germans, let me give these prisoners one boiled potato a day. I've got it. And remember, he had to feed the guards as well. There were about 10 guards. And so it happened. Max-- again, no other name, just Max-- [? Brau ?] or [? Braun, ?] I don't know-- Max, every afternoon supervising that everybody should get that boiled potato. I wouldn't be sitting here if not be fore Max. That was in February 19-- or March-- January, 1944. Food was nonexistent practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left Wustegiersdorf January the 11th.

speaker: interviewee
text: 11th, so that was just weeks later-- a week later, let's say. The weather was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were 200 of you  at that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two?

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in the barn. Because in a, in a different farm, another 200, and so on. So that farmer, Max, made sure that we get that boiled potato every single day. And that was something fantastic to go into the barn with that hot potato, right? First, you ate the peeling. You saved the peeling, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: For a half an hour, you ate the peeling. And you, you kept the potato warm. And then you bit into that hot potato-- that lifesaver, right? It was just unbelievable, what that farm-- for 10 long days, he supplied potatoes for 200 prisoners. Or if you were unlucky, you got a little potato. Or you know, there are potatoes and potatoes. But still, it was a lifeline for us, a boiled potato. Were all the other prisoners young like yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many like myself, yes, many like myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you stay there for so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: The weather did not allow us to continue with our march. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually, we were marched out from that farm. And there was a woman standing, which we never saw before, at the farmhouse, also a woman, a late 50s or 60-year-old woman. And she, she was crying. And she was saying, I should have given you two potatoes a day. And we speak in German. I told, her never mind. You saved our life. I said, never mind. You gave us a potato. You saved us. I don't know whether she heard me because of that clattering going out. We were already leaving the farm. So that was our experience the first few days marching through Silesia. Eventually, we went as far as Dessau. I mean, we had to stop in Friedland concentration camp. And those were terrible days. But eventually, we reached the railway station of Dessau. And there, we were put into open goods trains. And then Helmut, the guard, was running up and down the platform, looking for me. Imagine that. There already other hundreds of prisoners joined the train, not only the 500. And he found me. It was nearly dark. Ernest! Ernest! Ernst! Ernst! Ernst! And he threw me a round-- half of  a round loaf into the-- and I caught it. Certainly, they all went for it. I was left with very little. But still, what a mensch he was. He found me. Ernst! Ernst-- gave me bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- they, they tore it from me in seconds, these people, right, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out his surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never-- again, never-- can you imagine that? Not one of these wonderful people could I detect after the war, because just Helmut, finished. What interest [? to me was ?] his second name? I wasn't-- look, I was 19. I wasn't so practical yet, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not count on surviving anyway. How can you survive that hell? So why would you put in  practical? Blah, blah, blah, right? Tell me about the train journey from Dessau.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the train journey from Dessau was-- we were covered in snow. We had open wagons. And somewhere at the Elbe, this train stopped, because there was some bombardment going on-- some, some air raid somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stopped for the whole night on the Elbe. And there, we made really friends with the German guard. He was sitting in the corner on a stool from 10 to 12. That was his-- and then they became friends. Unbelievable chap he was. That's Helmut.

speaker: interviewee
text: The dialogue on the Elbe is unforgettable. He told me about his family. I told him about Aushchwitz. He didn't know. I told him about Birchenau. He didn't know. He didn't know. He told me about his family. His father was a dentist-- lost one leg in the First War, wonderful cellist. His mother was a devoted Nazi, worshiped the Fuhrer, and so on. And that went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Helmut at the time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Wermacht or SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wermacht. Wermacht. Wermacht. Wermacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was my friend, Helmut. And we arrived eventually, because somewhere nearer the place, nearer Belsen, that hundreds and thousands of prisoners thrown into a wagons. They were already infected with lice. And they, they were more dead than alive. So it was terrible. The last day of the journey was indescribable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived. And we met the commander, the commandant of Belsen, Josef Kramer, who gave me with his whip across my face. I was sure I lost my right eye. I was sure-- across the face with his whip, because I wanted to say something. Because he selected 10 people who would pull the dead and half dead from the wagons. And that-- and I didn't want to lose my friends, you know? You know, to lose a friend was the greatest tragedy. So I said something. He whipped me with his-- across the face. It [? swole ?] up right away. And I thought, I thought, my, my, my right eye [INAUDIBLE]. So the whole-- it was-- by then, it was two o'clock, three o'clock in the morning, you remember? And they had to pull, I mean, and to pile them up on tracks. And Kramer, Kramer was shouting, [GERMAN] That they should be piled up, right? Like matches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like matches-- and there were people still alive, many people still alive. I mean, my famous story-- I was sitting on top of one of these trucks, on top of all of these dead people and half dead people, right? I was sitting there. And that man sits up, looks in my eyes. He says, my name is Dr. Weiss. I am a physician from Budapest. I am not dead. I am just very weak. And back  he went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there were the dying and the dead in the truck on top of each other, in the thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you had arrived at Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, we knew. We knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard later that it was Josef Kramer who hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, Josef Kramer, who was the commandant of Auschwitz, you must remember, originally. He was transferred from Auschwitz to Belsen. And that was his holiday-- a sadist par excellence. You know he was hanged after the war? Karmer was hanged in Belsen.

words.text: he was hanged after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my [NON-ENGLISH], my welcome in the camp. Eventually, I lost contact with everybody. It was 3 o'clock in the morning, 4 o'clock in the morning. I was sitting on top of this, of, of, of, of hundreds of, of, of dead bodies, right? And we, we arrived at the gate of the camp. The driver got out of the truck and walked away. And Ernest Levy found himself alone at the gates of Belsen on top of the dead bodies, [NON-ENGLISH], totally  alone, no friend, no nothing. Where do you go? What do you do? The driver walked away finished. Only in the far distance could you hear shouting-- some appell went on or something. And in the very far distance, a fire was raging-- absolute hell. And I was sitting there on top of, of, of the dead bodies, right? Where do you go, what do you-- eventually, I climbed down. And I walked in through the gates of Belsen. It was my first voluntary walking into Belsen. And I was screaming. Joe, my friend, Joe-- I lost my friend. And was thousand-- hundreds of barracks-- no doors, no windows, just barracks, right? And blindly, I went into one barrack. And I said, Joe. He says, I'm here. So I went out under his blanket. And we were crying bitterly. But it was wonderful to find your friend in that situation. So that was-- Had Joe been on, on the truck that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. No, Joe was not selected by Kramer to go back to the ramp. I was selected by Kramer to go back on the ramp. And because I didn't want to lose my friend, I said, ah, ah-- back, you know? So I found Joe. Joel Orenz was a distant relation of mine. And so we woke up

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning. The sun was brightly shining through the doorless barrack. And we saw heaps of bodies-- heaps and heaps of bodies moving. The bodies were moving. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The bodies were moving. There were so many still alive. Can you imagine? That's how we woke up the first morning in Belsen-- and no food, no water, no nothing, no sanitation, nothing, no Germans, nothing, just the kapos. And you were left to perish there, right? And so after a week or two, we were taken away from Belsen. During that couple of weeks no Germans appeared, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, look, oh, yes, we saw occasionally German guards coming and going. But generally, they didn't care. They did not care. Because Belsen was not a labor camp. It was not an organized situation at all. It was just people arrived in such numbers, nobody care, right? We were just left there to perish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Despite the ghastly conditions, what sort of food supply existed?

speaker: interviewee
text: An occasional plate of soup-- an occasional plate of soup, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapos, the kapos brought it from somewhere. But the last weeks, no food, no water. The second time in Belsen-- no food, no water, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we're still at the beginning of February now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there for two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they needed people for working in a mine that they built in Hanover. Outside Hanover, they built a factory underground-- a munition, munition factory. And we were needed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were taken to that camp, to Hanover, outside Hanover. Ahlem I think it was called-- Hanover-Ahlem, which was, again, on the mercy of the kapos-- 10 days of hell, absolute hell. And we went to work to that underground factory. A large group of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And the night shift just came up-- remember, the Dutch people who worked during the night. They looked like dead. They actually all looked like dead. So they came up. We went down. We got a little bread, a little margarine. That was finished-- the whole day, finito, just, just to survive the day. And working under this condition, imagine no food, no water, really, and 500, 600 feet under the ground in the, in that, in that lack of air and-- undescribable hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- when you did not work, where were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a camp-- in a concentration camp-- a small concentration camp, where the kapos performed, night after night, performed atrocities to entertain us. It was terrible, terrible, really [INAUDIBLE], really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any Allied bombing during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. And so we had to leave Hanover. The camp was bombed. Yes, the camp was bombed. And the surroundings was bombed. And we had to leave Hanover. We were needed in Hildesheim, because the railway station in Hildesheim was bombed. And we had to go and work there. We are taken there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hildesheim, there was not a real concentration camp, right? That was probably [? Zurich ?] already. The, the National Theater was made into a camp, which was terrible, if you think. A beautiful, lovely place, torn into pieces by us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came the Allied bombing of, of Hildesheim on a Friday. I think it was the 11th of February. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were all running towards the outskirts of Hildesheim, because it was obvious the city was condemned to die-- to be destroyed. You could see it, this. And the whole population was running. The whole population was running towards the cemeteries and towards the outskirts of the city. Because you could already hear the fantastic noise of the bombing-- the bombers coming nearer to the city. It was undescribable-- fantastic. And then my famous incident-- how stupid one can be if you are German and you are commanded to do something. And you are  brainwashed. Can you-- I give you a scenario. Already, the, the bombs were falling. The population of the city were running, and screaming, and shouting towards the outskirts, right, to get away from the bombing. So were we. And  in the confusion, some of us went onto the pavement, certainly. And there is a German young soldier standing there on one leg with two crutches. And he shouts-- [GERMAN]. I, I said to him, you idiot. He looked at me. Oh, my god. I said, you idiot! I gave him-- imagine this, to be brainwashed to that extent. That was his greatest problem. The city is five minutes away from total destruction. Everybody is running and screaming and shouting [GERMAN]. And he said, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't let the Jews walk on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't let the Jews on the pavement, onto the pavement. I was just bypassing him. You idiot! And he looked in disbelief, you know? So that was Hildesheim. And then we had to leave Hildesheim with no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you, during this air raid, were you-- when everybody was running-- were you under guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, the guards were also running, everybody. That was chaos, chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they then collect the prisoners, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, we ended up in the Jewish cemetery, believe it or not. We ended up to take shelter in a Jewish-- ancient Jewish-- cemetery in Hildesheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to escape during--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet, not yet. That comes a bit later, but in Hildesheim, no, no. That we, we took shelter. But the city was destroyed, a beautiful city, totally destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sheltered in the cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not going-- we had nowhere to go back. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was way out-- back to Belsen, back to Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards marched you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Belsen-- and that night, on that night, some people tried to escape. One of them lives in Glasgow who was also shot. Four tried to escape. He was also shot, but only in his thigh. And he survived. And he woke up in Birmingham. Because the American Army was already coming. And they found him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you seen him since?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Yeah, he's my tailor. So that was the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue about what happened during that night.

speaker: interviewee
text: During that night, these four people tried to step out into the forest. And they were shot. And then we had to sleep in the out-- in the open, in the bitter cold. And they said, if you just sit up, you're shot at. You have to lie. Can you imagine that, in February?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was beginning of March, the night was, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning of March-- if you sit up, it's enough. You will be shot. Because there are not enough guards to control these people, right? So that was the only way out. If you sit up, you are shot at. You are dead. You have to lie in that cold. Imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this walk take to Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three days-- four days, yes, and then we arrived at the gates of Belsen, a completely different side of Belsen. And the were the prisoners start-- I remember holding on to the, to the, to the, to the fence there, an elderly man, like a ghost, a skeleton. And we could hear him saying, don't come in here. Don't. It's typhoid everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we marched [INAUDIBLE]. We marched in at the last few weeks in Belsen. The whole know-- the whole world knows the pictures and the movies, right? We had to pull the dead bodies to the pit day after day after day. There are young girls, hundreds of young girls, right, from all over the world, pulling them to the pit. It's very possible that I buried Anne Frank. It's very possible. She died in March, right? She died in March and just two or three few blocks away from me. It's very possible that I pulled her to the, to the pit. Because all day we had to do that until we were so weak, you sat down. And you said, enough, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sent down by a German officer, Wermacht officer. Because the, the, the center of the pit was fairly empty. Because he hadn't got the [NON-ENGLISH] to, to-- so [INAUDIBLE]. He send me down to pull the dead bodies towards the center. He had a gun hanging in his hand. And I went down. Needless to say, I couldn't manage. I mean, I was at the end of my [GERMAN], of my strength. They were all tangled up. I couldn't. And he was standing there on top of the pit, turning the other way, looking I don't know where towards, not, not bothering what I am doing or not doing. Eventually, I fell asleep. And it was nighttime. And I woke up. And I tried to get out of the pit. I couldn't. Hadn't had the strength. But the instinct, the life instinct, made me try and try again. And the little sleep also gave me a little energy. So I ended up on top of the pit with my two elbows holding on. And along comes somebody and says, [GERMAN]. And I look and say, [GERMAN]. He gets a hold of me and pulls me out. That's why I'm sitting here. Who it was, what it was, I don't know. Eventually, I dragged myself back to the, to the, to the block.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how I'm alive. He pulled me out from the grave, because I spent many hours in that grave. I fell asleep. It was late night when I woke up. Comes along somebody at that second, because I couldn't afford much longer. And he said, [GERMAN], you know? Are you alive or are you dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you alive, or are you dead? Say, I'm, I'm alive. So he pulled me out from the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never found out if it was a prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- never-- prisoner, or a German, or who was that coming along? If he comes along two, five, 10 seconds later, I, I, I drop back into the pit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that German officer again that directed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never. He was away by then-- finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a few days before-- what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been a couple of weeks before the liberation. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: During those two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that-- still pulling the dead bodies. One day, I myself lied in the dust. I said, enough is enough. And all day, they were pulling the dead bodies over me. Imagine that-- all day stepping over me, on my head, my hair, [INAUDIBLE]-- all day. I said, no, I'm not moving. I had enough. I can't. I'm finished. You know, no eating, no drinking for weeks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The life instinct again works. You know, you got-- you get up. And you fight, right? The last days, you picked up anything from the, from the dust. You, you started biting it, just too tragic for words. But really, I mean, I don't want to go really into these details what happened the last week in Belsen. It's beyond description. There was no organization at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, so much so that the last week, the Hungarian guards took over Belsen. And the Germans disappeared. Many disappeared into blocks and put on the striped pajamas from the dead prisoners to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- despite the tragic circumstances, was there any knowledge of the advancing armies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Yes. Yes, There was. There was. But as far as I'm concerned, I would have held out maybe one more day-- the most. So when the army, the British liberating army, eventually entered the camp, I couldn't stand on my feet anymore. I was lying in the dust near a fence. And I managed to lift up my head and lift up my arm and do ah! That was my, that was my welcome. That was my liberation on the 15th of April, '44-- the last minute.
